en.extract:
The Asian houbara (Chlamydotis macqueenii), also known as MacQueen's bustard, is a large bird in the bustard family. It is native to the desert and steppe regions of Asia, west from the Sinai Peninsula extending across Iran and further north toward Kazakhstan and Mongolia. In the 19th century, vagrants were found as far west of their range as Great Britain. Populations have decreased by 20–50% between 1984 and 2004 mainly due to hunting and changes in land-use. The Asian houbara is a partial latitudinal migrant while the African houbara (C. undulata) is more sedentary. These two species are the only members of the genus Chlamydotis. The Asian houbara used to be regarded as a subspecies of the African houbara.


Taxonomy

Otis macqueenii was proposed by John Edward Gray in 1834 for a bustard from India drawn by Thomas Hardwicke.
It was long regarded a subspecies of the African houbara Chlamydotis undulata. It was classified as a distinct species in 2003.
The genus name Chlamydotis is from Ancient Greek khlamus, a horseman's cloak with weights sewn into the corners, and otis, bustard.
The Asian houbara is larger than the African houbara and much paler. The feathers on the top of the head include some long and curved feathers which are white or black with white bases. In the houbara, these crest feathers are all white and the difference is evident during the display of the male. The lack of intermediate forms in the region where the ranges of the Asian houbara and the African houbara meet, presumed to be in the Nile valley, differences in morphology and display behaviour led to their being elevated to full species. The African houbara now refers only to the North African population, included as the nominate subspecies C. undulata undulata and a small population on the Canary Islands (C. u. fuertaventurae).
Estimates based on the divergence of mitochondrial DNA sequence suggest that the species separated from the common ancestors of C. u. undulata and C. u. fuertaventurae nearly 430,000 years ago. This divergence may have begun 900,000 years ago, at a time of extreme aridity. The wide dispersal abilities of the Asian houbara ensure that its genes are more well mixed unlike the geographically structured genetic patterns shown by the African houbara.


Description
This medium-sized bustard is about 65 cm (26 in) long with a 140 cm (55 in) wingspan. It is brown above and white below, with black stripes down the sides of the neck. In flight, the long wings show large areas of black and brown on the flight feathers and a white patch at the base of the primaries. From below the wing is mostly white with a black trailing edge. Sexes are similar, but the female is smaller and paler above.
Males and females are nearly identical in plumage but males are slightly larger than females. A study of the morphometrics of the Asian houbara from Pakistan based on about 79 individuals of known sex showed that the males were 9–15% larger than females on most measurements. The use of discriminant analysis allowed correct identification of the sexes based on morphometrics in about 99% of the cases.


Distribution and habitat
The Asian houbara occurs from the east of the Sinai Peninsula to the Caspian Sea and extending east to the Gobi Desert in Mongolia. Birds from the northern populations winter further south in Pakistan, mainly in western Balochistan, and in the dry arid zone of western India. Vagrants have historically been found as far west and north as Britain and as far south as northern Kerala. A bustard was shot in 1847 at Lincolnshire, Yorkshire in 1898, and another in Aberdeenshire in 1898 all in the month of October. Possibly the last of these vagrants visited Suffolk in November–December 1962. The Asian houbara breeds in deserts and other very arid sandy areas. Its habitat in Saudi Arabia is very dependent on good vegetation cover and tended to be found in areas with dense growth of scrub vegetation, particularly Capparis spinosa. A study in the steppes of Iran found that nest sites were chosen mainly in locations with high densities of insect prey which in turn were related to vegetation characteristics.


Migration
The migrations of the Asian houbara have been tracked using satellite transmitters. Mongolian birds leave the wintering areas in Afghanistan and Pakistan from mid to late March and arrive in their breeding grounds after about two months of flying, taking a path that avoids the high mountains of the Himalayas. They fly about 220 km (140 mi) a day and cover a total of 4,400 km (2,700 mi) with stopovers along the path. They spend about four months in their breeding territories before setting off again and reach their winter grounds from October to December.
Satellite tracking of 48 individual houbaras across multiple migrations showed that this species uses the local temperature to time their spring migration departure.


Behaviour and ecology

The male houbara displays initially with the neck upright and the feathers on the base of the neck erected. A few feathers on the head are also erected while walking slowly, with one foot moved carefully and placed just ahead of the other. This is followed by a more vigorous phase of running either in a line or in a circle around a few bushes while the neck is tucked back into an "S". The neck feathers are erected and cover the head. The feet are raised in a measured gait and the neck is swayed from side to side. A low sound of breathing may be heard but only at close range. Males will call during display and if there are no potential mates, the display may be repeated. When a mate appears to be receptive, the male puffs up the black feathers on the sides of the neck so that it appears like a black collar or ruff and walks towards the female while twisting his body from side to side. The males mate with multiple females and after mating, the female alone builds the nest and incubates. The clutch consists of 2–4 eggs laid in a bare scrape on the ground. The eggs hatch after about 23 days and as in all bustards, the nidifugous chicks leave the nest immediately after hatching and follow the mother which picks insects and passes them to the chicks with her beak. The young fledge in about 30 days but remain close to their mother for several months.
When pursued by falcons (such as the saker falcon or peregrine falcon) in falconry, the bustard rises into the air and spirals to avoid being struck. It has been claimed that it also defends itself by defecating on the falcon, the sticky green faeces causing the falcon to crash to the ground with wings stuck.<ref name=fbi>A., Salim; Ripley, D. S. (1980). Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan. Vol. 2. Megapodes to Crab Plover (2nd ed.). New Delhi: Oxford University Press. pp. 191–193. ISBN 978-0-19-565506-3.</ref>
This species is omnivorous taking seeds, berries, insects and other invertebrates. They do not drink water and obtain all the moisture they need from their diet. Tenebrionid beetles were found to be especially numerous in one study. Plant material makes up more of their diet during the non-breeding season.


Threats
The Asian houbara was once nearly hunted to near-extinction in the Middle East by Arab falconers, hunters and poachers. It was considered great sport in colonial India, especially to hunt tiloor (the local name) from camel back. The bird would be approached in narrowing circles and on close approach the bustard would squat on the ground and conceal itself. The introduction of jeeps and guns however led to a drastic decline in the population of the species. Hunts in some parts of Pakistan have been organized for wealthy Arabs who purchase permits to hunt a limited number of birds but routinely exceed quotas. The meat of this bustard is considered in the Arab world to be an aphrodisiac and a diuretic according to another source.
Rapid population declines of about 50% were seen in their breeding grounds in Kazakhstan between 1998 and 2002 and thought to be due to hunting, especially in their winter grounds.
Annual declines over a ten-year period across Asia were estimated at around 27–30% in 2004. The main threat to the Asian houbara is degradation of semi-desert habitat by the introduction of agriculture and by infrastructure development such as roads and electricity, which are responsible for increased mortality of birds. In addition, power line collisions are a significant and previously underreported threat along the Central Asian Flyway migration route in Uzbekistan. A 2024 conservation project conducted in the Abbas Abad Wildlife Refuge in central Iran found that installing brightly colored spirals diverters along a high-risk  power line eliminated reported collisions with Asian Houbara over a one-year period.
They also are at considerable risk during migration from heavy poaching as well as a lack of suitable habitats along their migration routes due to development.
It is very sensitive to disturbance by humans and livestock when nesting. However, sheep grazing does not disturb the Asian Houbara's non-breeding habitats.


Conservation
Conservation efforts were made across the region after the 1970s with international conservation organizations working along with local governments. Some captive breeding facilities were created including one in Saudi Arabia in 1986 and have been successful in captive breeding since the late 1990s, initially by incubating eggs collected from the wild and later entirely in captivity using artificial insemination. Captive-bred birds are considerably more inbred and may be susceptible to diseases.
Being migratory species, it is important that captive-bred bustards undertake similar migrations as wild birds. Comparing the migrations of captive and wild birds using satellite telemetry, it was found that captive-bred individuals started autumn migration later and wintered closer to the breeding grounds than wild individuals. The surviving captive-bred bustards were also faithful in their wintering locations in subsequent years. As migration has a genetic component, it is important to consider migratory population structure, as well as natal and release-site fidelity, during captive breeding management of this species.
The Asian houbara is protected in the United Arab Emirates. In February 2019, 50 individuals were released into the Arabian Desert in Al Ain Region in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi, to help conserve the species and increase its numbers in the wild.


References


External links

National Wildlife Research Centre, Saudi Arabia
BirdLife species factsheet for Chlamydotis macqueenii
"Chlamydotis macqueenii". Avibase. 
Interactive range map of Chlamydotis macqueenii at IUCN Red List
Audio recordings of MacQueen's bustard on Xeno-canto.

The '''Asian houbara''' ('''''Chlamydotis macqueenii'''''), also known as '''MacQueen's bustard''', is a large [[bird]] in the [[bustard]] family. It is native to the desert and [[steppe]] regions of [[Asia]], west from the [[Sinai Peninsula]] extending across [[Iran]] and further north toward [[Kazakhstan]] and [[Mongolia]]. In the 19th century, [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]]s were found as far west of their range as [[Great Britain]]. Populations have decreased by 20–50% between 1984 and 2004 mainly due to hunting and changes in land-use. The Asian houbara is a partial latitudinal [[Animal migration|migrant]] while the [[African houbara]] (''C. undulata'') is more sedentary. These two species are the only members of the genus ''[[Chlamydotis]]''. The Asian houbara used to be regarded as a subspecies of the African houbara.

==Taxonomy==
[[File:Otis macqueenii Hardwicke.jpg|thumb|upright|Lithograph from ''Illustrations of Indian Zoology'' (1832)]]
''Otis macqueenii'' was proposed by [[John Edward Gray]] in 1834 for a bustard from [[India]] drawn by [[Thomas Hardwicke]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gray |first1=J. E. |year=1834 |title=Illustrations of Indian Zoology; Chiefly Selected from the Collection of Major-General Hardwicke, F.R.S. Volume 2 |location=London |publisher=Treuttel, Würtz, Treuttel, Jun. and Richter |page=Plate 47 |chapter=MacQueen's bustard ''Otis macqueenii''. Gray |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/IllustrationsOfIndianZoology2/page/n101}}</ref>
It was long regarded a [[subspecies]] of the [[African houbara]] ''Chlamydotis undulata''.<ref>{{cite book |author=Ali, S. & Ripley, S. D. |year=1983 |title=A Pictorial Guide to the Birds of the Indian Subcontinent |publisher=Bombay Natural History Society |location=Bombay |chapter=''Chlamydotis undulata'' |page=106, Plate 37}}</ref> It was classified as a distinct [[species]] in 2003.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Knox |first1=A. G.  |last2=Collinson |first2=M. |last3=Helbig |first3=A. J. |last4=Parkin |first4=D. T. |last5=Sangster |first5=G. |year=2002 |title=Taxonomic recommendations for British birds |journal=Ibis |volume=144 |issue= 4|pages=707–710 |doi=10.1046/j.1474-919X.2002.00110.x |s2cid=82531549 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

The genus name ''Chlamydotis'' is from [[Ancient Greek]] {{Lang|grc-Latn|khlamus}}, a horseman's cloak with weights sewn into the corners, and {{Lang|grc-Latn|otis}}, bustard.<ref name=job>{{cite book |last=Jobling |first=J. A. |year=2010 |title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n102 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |pages=102, 235}}</ref>

The Asian houbara is larger than the African houbara and much paler. The feathers on the top of the head include some long and curved feathers which are white or black with white bases. In the houbara, these crest feathers are all white and the difference is evident during the display of the male. The lack of intermediate forms in the region where the ranges of the Asian houbara and the African houbara meet, presumed to be in the [[Nile valley]], differences in morphology and display behaviour led to their being elevated to full species. The African houbara now refers only to the North African population, included as the nominate subspecies ''C. undulata undulata'' and a small population on the Canary Islands (''C. u. fuertaventurae'').<ref name=split>{{cite journal |first1=P. |last1=Gaucher |first2=P. |last2=Paillat |first3=C. |last3=Chappuis |first4=M. |last4=Saint Jalme |first5=F. |last5=Lotfikhah |first6=M. |last6=Wink |title=Taxonomy of the Houbara Bustard ''Chlamydotis undulata'' subspecies considered on the basis of sexual display and genetic divergence |journal=Ibis |volume=138 |issue=2 |pages=273–282 |year=1996 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1996.tb04339.x |url=https://www.uni-heidelberg.de/institute/fak14/ipmb/phazb/pubwink/1996/11_1996.pdf}}</ref>

Estimates based on the divergence of [[mitochondrial DNA]] sequence suggest that the species separated from the common ancestors of ''C. u. undulata'' and ''C. u. fuertaventurae'' nearly 430,000 years ago.<ref name=Idaghdour2004>{{cite journal |first1=Y. |last1=Idaghdour |first2=D. |last2=Broderick |first3=A. |last3=Korrida |first4=F. |last4=Chbel |title=Mitochondrial control region diversity of the Houbara Bustard ''Chlamydotis undulata'' complex and genetic structure along the Atlantic seaboard of North Africa |doi=10.1046/j.1365-294X.2003.02039.x |pmid=14653787 |journal=Molecular Ecology |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=43–54 |year=2004 |s2cid=25591653}}</ref> This divergence may have begun 900,000 years ago, at a time of extreme aridity.<ref name=Korrida2014>{{cite journal |first1=A. |last1=Korrida |first2=M. |last2=Schweizer |title=Diversification across the Palaearctic desert belt throughout the Pleistocene: phylogeographic history of the Houbara–Macqueen's bustard complex (Otididae: ''Chlamydotis'') as revealed by mitochondrial DNA |doi=10.1111/jzs.12036 |journal=Journal of Zoological Systematics and Evolutionary Research |volume=52 |issue=1 |pages=65–74 |year=2014 |doi-access=free}}</ref> The wide dispersal abilities of the Asian houbara ensure that its genes are more well mixed unlike the geographically structured genetic patterns shown by the African houbara.<ref name=Pitra2004>{{cite journal |first1=C. |last1=Pitra |first2=M.-A. |last2=D'Aloia |first3=D. |last3=Lieckfeldt |first4=O. |last4=Combreau |title=Genetic variation across the current range of the Asian houbara bustard (''Chlamydotis undulata macqueenii'') |journal=Conservation Genetics |year=2004 |volume=5 |issue=2 |pages=205–215 |doi=10.1023/B:COGE.0000030004.51398.28 |s2cid=28253332}}</ref>

==Description==
This medium-sized bustard is about {{cvt|65|cm}} long with a {{cvt|140|cm}} wingspan. It is brown above and white below, with black stripes down the sides of the neck. In flight, the long wings show large areas of black and brown on the flight feathers and a white patch at the base of the primaries. From below the wing is mostly white with a black trailing edge. Sexes are similar, but the female is smaller and paler above.<ref name=pcr>{{cite book |last1=Rasmussen |first1=P.C. |first2=J.C. |last2=Anderton |year=2005 |title=Birds of South Asia. The Ripley Guide |volume=2 |pages=148–149 |publisher=Smithsonian Institution and Lynx Edicions |place=Washington, DC and Barcelona |isbn=978-84-96553-85-9}}</ref>

Males and females are nearly identical in plumage but males are slightly larger than females. A study of the morphometrics of the Asian houbara from [[Pakistan]] based on about 79 individuals of known sex showed that the males were 9–15% larger than females on most measurements. The use of [[Linear discriminant analysis|discriminant analysis]] allowed correct identification of the sexes based on morphometrics in about 99% of the cases.<ref name=Jarrett1999>{{cite journal |last1=Jarrett |first1=N. S. |last2=Warren |first2=S. M. |year=1999 |title=A preliminary guide for age and sex determination of the Houbara Bustard ''Chlamydotis undulata macqueenii'' |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=96 |issue=1 |pages=28–41 |url=https://archive.org/details/journalofbombay961999bomb/page/28}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The Asian houbara occurs from the east of the Sinai Peninsula to the [[Caspian Sea]] and extending east to the [[Gobi Desert]] in Mongolia.<ref name=split/> Birds from the northern populations winter further south in Pakistan, mainly in western Balochistan,<ref name=Mian1983>{{cite journal |last1=Mian |first1=A. |first2=M. I. |last2=Surahio |year=1983 |title=Biology of Houbara Bustard (''Chlamydotis undulata macqueenii'') with reference to Baluchistan |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=80 |pages=111–118|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48743699}}</ref> and in the dry arid zone of western India. Vagrants have historically been found as far west and north as Britain and as far south as northern Kerala.<ref name=Sashikumar1989>{{cite journal |last=Sashikumar |first=C. |year=1989 |title=Houbara Bustard ''Chlamydotis undulata'': a rare record from Kerala. |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=86 |issue=1 |page=101|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48710445}}</ref><ref name=pcr/> A bustard was shot in 1847 at [[Lincolnshire]], [[Yorkshire]] in 1898, and another in [[Aberdeenshire]] in 1898 all in the month of October.<ref name=Howse1894>{{cite journal |last=Howse |first=R. |title=On the occurrence on the North-East Coast of Yorkshire of a Ruffed-Bustard, ''Otis houbara'', Gmelin, commonly known as Macqueen's Bustard, Otis Macqueenii, J.E.Gray |journal=Natural History Transactions of Northumberland, Durham and Newcastle-on-Tyne |volume=11 |year=1894 |pages=345–350 |url=https://archive.org/stream/transactionsofna9094natu#page/348/mode/2up}}</ref><ref name=Cordeaux1896>{{cite journal |last=Cordeaux |first=J. |title=Occurrence of MacQueen's Bustard in East Yorkshire |pages=323–324 |year=1896 |journal=The Naturalist |url=https://archive.org/stream/mobot31753002473665#page/322/mode/2up/}}</ref><ref name=Thorburn1916>{{cite book |last=Thorburn |first=A. |title=British Birds |volume=4 |publisher=Longmans, Green and Co. |place=London |year=1916 |url=https://archive.org/stream/britishbirds04thor#page/2/mode/2up |page=2}}</ref> Possibly the last of these vagrants visited [[Suffolk]] in November–December 1962.<ref name=Jobson2004>{{cite journal |first1=G. J. |last1=Jobson |first2=B. J. |last2=Small |title=From the Rarities Committee's files: The Macqueen's Bustard in Suffolk in 1962 |journal=British Birds |volume=97 |year=2004 |pages=68–72 |url=http://britishbirds.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/article_files/V97/V97_N02/V97_N02_P068_072_A002.pdf}}</ref> The Asian houbara breeds in deserts and other very arid sandy areas. Its habitat in [[Saudi Arabia]] is very dependent on good vegetation cover and tended to be found in areas with dense growth of scrub vegetation, particularly ''[[Capparis spinosa]]''.<ref name=Launay1997>{{cite journal |first1=F. |last1=Launay |first2=D. |last2=Roshier |first3=R. |last3=Loughland |first4= S.J. |last4=Aspinall |title=Habitat use by houbara bustard (''Chlamydotis undulata macqueenii'') in arid shrubland in the United Arab Emirates |journal=Journal of Arid Environments |volume=35 |issue=1 |year=1997 |pages=111–121 |doi=10.1006/jare.1995.0136 |bibcode=1997JArEn..35..111L}}</ref><ref name=Osborne1997>{{cite journal |first1=Patrick E. |last1=Osborne |first2=Frédéric |last2=Launay |first3=Derek |last3=Gliddon |title=Wintering habitat use by houbara bustards ''Chlamydotis undulata'' in Abu Dhabi and implications for management |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=81 |issue=1–2 |year=1997 |pages=51–56 |doi=10.1016/s0006-3207(96)00157-7}}</ref><ref name=vanHeezik1999>{{cite journal |first1=Y. |last1=Van Heezik |author-link1=Yolanda van Heezik |first2=P. J. |last2=Seddon |title=Seasonal changes in habitat use by Houbara Bustards ''Chlamydotis [undulata] macqueenii'' in northern Saudi Arabia |journal=Ibis |year=1999 |volume=141 |issue=2 |pages=208–215 |url=http://www.nwrc.gov.sa/NWRC_ENG/Publications_files/1-1999-003.pdf |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1999.tb07543.x}}</ref> A study in the steppes of [[Iran]] found that nest sites were chosen mainly in locations with high densities of insect prey which in turn were related to vegetation characteristics.<ref name=Aghanajafizadeh2012>{{cite journal |first1=Shirin |last1=Aghanajafizadeh |first2=M. R. |last2=Hemami |first3=F. |last3=Heydari |title=Nest-site selection by the Asian Houbara Bustard, ''Chlamydotis macqueenii'', in the steppe of Harat, Iran |journal=Zoology in the Middle East |volume=57 |issue=1 |year=2012 |doi=10.1080/09397140.2012.10648958 |pages=11–18 |s2cid=88168591}}</ref>

=== Migration ===
The migrations of the Asian houbara have been tracked using satellite transmitters. Mongolian birds leave the wintering areas in [[Afghanistan]] and Pakistan from mid to late March and arrive in their breeding grounds after about two months of flying, taking a path that avoids the high mountains of the [[Himalayas]]. They fly about {{cvt|220|km}} a day and cover a total of {{cvt|4400|km}} with stopovers along the path. They spend about four months in their breeding territories before setting off again and reach their winter grounds from October to December.<ref>{{cite journal |first1=C. |last1=Tourenq |first2=O. |last2=Combreau |first3=M. |last3=Lawrence |first4=F. |last4=Launay |year=2004 |title=Migration patterns of four Asian Houbara ''Chlamydotis macqueenii'' wintering in the Cholistan Desert, Punjab, Pakistan |journal=Bird Conservation International |volume=14 |pages=1–10 |doi=10.1017/S0959270904000012 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Judas2006>{{cite journal |first1=J. |last1=Judas |first2=O. |last2=Combreau |first3=M. |last3=Lawrence |first4=M. |last4=Saleh |first5=F. |last5=Launay |first6=G. |last6=Xingyi |title=Migration and range use of Asian Houbara Bustard ''Chlamydotis macqueenii'' breeding in the Gobi Desert, China, revealed by satellite tracking |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2006.00546.x |journal=Ibis |volume=148 |issue=2 |pages=343–351 |year=2006}}</ref>

Satellite tracking of 48 individual houbaras across multiple migrations showed that this species uses the local temperature to time their spring migration departure.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Burnside|first1=R. J.|last2=Salliss |first2=D. |last3=Collar |first3=N. J. |last4=Dolman |first4=P. M. |date=2021 |title=Birds use individually consistent temperature cues to time their migration departure |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=118 |issue=28 |article-number=e2026378118 |doi=10.1073/pnas.2026378118 |pmid=34260383 |pmc=8285904 |bibcode=2021PNAS..11826378B |doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology ==
[[File:Chlamydotis macqueenii 1921.jpg|thumb|Illustration of a male in partial display with the ruff or collar erected]]
The male houbara displays initially with the neck upright and the feathers on the base of the neck erected. A few feathers on the head are also erected while walking slowly, with one foot moved carefully and placed just ahead of the other. This is followed by a more vigorous phase of running either in a line or in a circle around a few bushes while the neck is tucked back into an "S". The neck feathers are erected and cover the head. The feet are raised in a measured gait and the neck is swayed from side to side. A low sound of breathing may be heard but only at close range. Males will call during display and if there are no potential mates, the display may be repeated. When a mate appears to be receptive, the male puffs up the black feathers on the sides of the neck so that it appears like a black collar or ruff and walks towards the female while twisting his body from side to side.<ref name=split/><ref name=Launay1990>{{cite journal |first1=F. |last1=Launay |first2=P. |last2=Paillat |title=A behavioural repertoire of the adult houbara bustard (''Chlamydotis undulata macqueenii'') |journal=Revue d'Écologie – la Terre et la Vie |volume=45 |year=1990 |pages=65–88 |doi=10.3406/revec.1990.1983 |s2cid=55423007 |url=http://nwrc.gov.sa/NWRC_ARB/mnshwrat_files/1-1990-011.pdf}}</ref> The males mate with multiple females and after mating, the female alone builds the nest and incubates. The clutch consists of 2–4 eggs laid in a bare scrape on the ground. The eggs hatch after about 23 days and as in all bustards, the nidifugous chicks leave the nest immediately after hatching and follow the mother which picks insects and passes them to the chicks with her beak. The young fledge in about 30 days but remain close to their mother for several months.<ref name=ontogeny>{{cite journal |first1=Y. |last1=Van Heezik |author-link1=Yolanda van Heezik |first2=P. J. |last2=Seddon |title=Ontogeny of behavior of hand-reared and hen-reared captive Houbara Bustards |journal=Zoo Biology |volume=17 |issue=3 |pages=245–255 |year=1998 |url=http://nwrc.gov.sa/NWRC_ARB/mnshwrat_files/1-1998-005.pdf |doi=10.1002/(sici)1098-2361(1998)17:3<245::aid-zoo7>3.0.co;2-9}}</ref>

When pursued by falcons (such as the [[saker falcon]] or [[peregrine falcon]]) in falconry, the bustard rises into the air and spirals to avoid being struck. It has been claimed that it also defends itself by defecating on the falcon, the sticky green faeces causing the falcon to crash to the ground with wings stuck.<<ref>{{cite journal |author-link=Douglas Craven Phillott|author=Phillott, D. C. |year=1906 |title=Note on the Houbara or Bastard Bustard (Houbara Macqueenii) |journal= Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal |volume=2 |pages=449–454 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/31507413}}</ref>ref name=fbi>{{Cite book |first1=Salim |last1=A. |first2=D. S. |last2=Ripley |title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan |volume=2. Megapodes to Crab Plover |edition=2nd |year=1980 |pages=191–193 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New Delhi |isbn=978-0-19-565506-3}}</ref>

This species is omnivorous taking seeds, berries, insects and other invertebrates. They do not drink water and obtain all the moisture they need from their diet. [[Tenebrionidae|Tenebrionid beetles]] were found to be especially numerous in one study.<ref name=Tigar2000>{{cite journal |first1=B. J. |last1=Tigar |first2=P. E. |last2=Osborne |journal=Ibis |volume=142 |issue=3 |title=Invertebrate diet of the Houbara Bustard ''Chlamydotis'' [''undulata''] ''macqueenii'' in Abu Dhabi from calibrated faecal analysis |year=2000 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2000.tb04443.x |pages=466–475}}</ref> Plant material makes up more of their diet during the non-breeding season.<ref name=Nadeem2004>{{cite journal |last1=Nadeem |first1=M.S. |last2=Ali |first2=F. |last3=Akhtar |first3=S. |year=2004 |title=Diet of houbara bustard ''Chlamydotis undulata'' in Punjab, Pakistan |journal=Forktail |volume=20 |pages=91–93 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/234140491}}</ref>

==Threats==
The Asian houbara was once nearly hunted to near-extinction in the [[Middle East]] by Arab [[falcon]]ers, hunters and [[Poaching|poachers]]. It was considered great sport in colonial India, especially to hunt ''tiloor'' (the local name) from camel back. The bird would be approached in narrowing circles and on close approach the bustard would squat on the ground and conceal itself.<ref name=Baker1921>{{cite book |last=Baker |first=E.C. Stuart |title=The game-birds of India, Burma and Ceylon. Snipe, bustards and sand-grouse |volume=2 |year=1921 |publisher=Bombay Natural History Society |location=Bombay |pages=186–197 |url=https://archive.org/stream/gamebirdsofindia02bake#page/186/mode/2up}}</ref> The introduction of jeeps and guns however led to a drastic decline in the population of the species. Hunts in some parts of Pakistan have been organized for wealthy Arabs who purchase permits to hunt a limited number of birds but routinely exceed quotas. The meat of this bustard is considered in the Arab world to be an [[aphrodisiac]] and a [[diuretic]] according to another source.<ref name=Weaver1992>{{Cite magazine |last=Weaver |first=M. A. |magazine=The New Yorker |date=1992 |title=Hunting with the Sheikhs |pages=51–64 |url=http://archives.newyorker.com/?i=1992-12-14#folio=050 |url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |author=Coniff, R. |url=http://www.takepart.com/article/2014/02/07/sex-drive-rich-men-killing-bird |title=These birds are dying so rich, powerful men can improve their sex lives |date=2014 |access-date=4 January 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160111173034/http://www.takepart.com/article/2014/02/07/sex-drive-rich-men-killing-bird|archive-date=11 January 2016}}</ref>

Rapid population declines of about 50% were seen in their breeding grounds in Kazakhstan between 1998 and 2002 and thought to be due to hunting, especially in their winter grounds.<ref name=Tourenq2004>{{cite journal |first1=C. |last1=Tourenq |first2=O. |last2=Combreau |first3=S. B. |last3=Pole |first4=M. |last4=Lawrence |first5=V. S. |last5=Ageyev |first6=A. A. |last6=Karpov |first7=F. |last7=Launay |title=Monitoring of Asian houbara bustard ''Chlamydotis macqueenii'' populations in Kazakhstan reveals dramatic decline |journal=Oryx |volume=38 |issue=1 |pages=62–67 |year=2004 |doi=10.1017/S0030605304000109 |doi-access=free}}</ref>
Annual declines over a ten-year period across Asia were estimated at around 27–30% in 2004. The main threat to the Asian houbara is degradation of semi-desert habitat by the introduction of agriculture and by infrastructure development such as roads and electricity, which are responsible for increased mortality of birds. In addition, power line collisions are a significant and previously underreported threat along the [[Central Asian Flyway]] migration route in [[Uzbekistan]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kashkarov |first1=R. |last2=Shernazarov |first2=E. |last3=Baumbach |first3=F. |last4=Dmitrenok |first4=M. |last5=Jbour |first5=S. |title=Avian powerline mortalities including Asian Houbaras (''Chlamydotis macqueenii'') on the Central Asian flyway in Uzbekistan |journal=Sandgrouse |volume=37 |pages=161–168 |year=2015 |url=https://osme.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Avian-powerline-mortalities-including-Asian-Houbaras-Chlamydotis-macqueenii-on-the-Central-Asian-flyway-in-Uzbekistan.pdf}}</ref> A 2024 conservation project conducted in the Abbas Abad Wildlife Refuge in central [[Iran]] found that installing brightly colored spirals diverters along a high-risk  power line eliminated reported collisions with Asian Houbara over a one-year period.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Ebrahimi, I. |author2=Kazemi, F. |author3=Agha Ebrahimi, M. A. |year=2024|title=Effectiveness of spiral diverters in preventing Asian Houbara power line collisions in Abbas Abad Wildlife Refuge, Iran |journal=Ecopersia |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=407–414 |doi=10.22034/ECOPERSIA.12.4.405}}</ref>

They also are at considerable risk during migration from heavy poaching as well as a lack of suitable habitats along their migration routes due to development.<ref name=Tourenq2005>{{cite journal |last1=Tourenq |first1=C. |first2=O. |last2=Combreau |first3=M. |last3=Lawrence |first4=Serguei B. |last4=Pole |first5=Andrew |last5=Spalton |first6=Gao |last6=Xinji |first7=Mohammed |last7=Al Baidani |first8=F. |last8=Launay |title=Alarming houbara bustard population trends in Asia |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=121 |issue=1 |year=2005 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2004.03.031 |pages=1–8}}</ref>
It is very sensitive to disturbance by humans and livestock when nesting.<ref name=Lavee1988>{{cite journal |first=Daphna |last=Lavee |title=Why is the Houbara ''Chlamydotis undulata macqueenii'' still an endangered species in Israel? |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=45 |issue=1 |year=1988 |pages=47–54 |doi=10.1016/0006-3207(88)90051-1}}</ref> However, sheep grazing does not disturb the Asian Houbara's non-breeding habitats.<ref name=Goriup1997>{{cite journal |first=Paul D. |last=Goriup |title=The world status of the Houbara Bustard ''Chlamydotis undulata'' |journal=Bird Conservation International |volume=7 |issue=4 |year=1997 |pages=373–397 |doi=10.1017/S0959270900001714 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Koshkin2014>{{cite journal |first1=M. A. |last1=Koshkin |first2=N. J. |last2=Collar |first3=P. M. |last3=Dolman |title=Do sheep affect distribution and habitat of Asian Houbara ''Chlamydotis macqueenii''? |journal=Journal of Arid Environments |volume=103 |year=2014 |pages=53–62 |doi=10.1016/j.jaridenv.2014.01.002 |bibcode=2014JArEn.103...53K}}</ref>

== Conservation ==
Conservation efforts were made across the region after the 1970s with international conservation organizations working along with local governments. Some captive breeding facilities were created including one in Saudi Arabia in 1986 and have been successful in captive breeding since the late 1990s,<ref name=Gelinaud1997>{{cite journal |first1=G. |last1=Gelinaud |first2=O. |last2=Combreau |first3=P.J. |last3=Seddon |title=First breeding by captive-bred houbara bustards introduced in central Saudi Arabia |journal=Journal of Arid Environments |year=1997 |volume=35 |issue=3 |pages=527–534 |url=http://www.nwrc.gov.sa/NWRC_ENG/Publications_files/1-1997-006.pdf |doi=10.1006/jare.1996.0155 |bibcode=1997JArEn..35..527G}}</ref> initially by incubating eggs collected from the wild and later entirely in captivity using artificial insemination.<ref name=Jalme1994>{{cite journal |first1=M. |last1=Saint Jalme |first2=P. |last2=Gaucher |first3=P. |last3=Paillat |title=Artificial insemination in Houbara bustards (''Chlamydotis undulata''): influence of the number of spermatozoa and insemination frequency on fertility and ability to hatch |journal=Journal of Reproduction and Fertility |year=1994 |volume=100 |issue=1 |pages=93–103 |doi=10.1530/jrf.0.1000093 |pmid=8182618 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Captive-bred birds are considerably more inbred and may be susceptible to diseases.<ref name=ontogeny/><ref name=Greth1993>{{cite journal |first1=Arnaud |last1=Greth |first2=Bruno |last2=Andral |first3=Hermann |last3=Gerbermann |first4=Marc |last4=Vassart |first5=Helga |last5=Gerlach |first6=Frédéric |last6=Launay |title=Chlamydiosis in a captive group of Houbara Bustards (''Chlamydotis undulata'') |journal=Avian Diseases |volume=37 |issue=4 |year=1993 |pages=1117–1120 |doi=10.2307/1591923 |jstor=1591923 |pmid=8141742}}</ref><ref name=Samour1996>{{cite journal |first1=J.H. |last1=Samour |first2=O-R |last2=Kaaden |first3=U. |last3=Wernery |first4=T. A. |last4=Bailey |title=An epornitic of Avian Pox in Houbara Bustards: (''Chlamydotis undulata macqueenii'') |doi=10.1111/j.1439-0450.1996.tb00316.x |pmid=8779810 |journal=Journal of Veterinary Medicine, Series B |volume=43 |issue=1–10 |pages=287–292 |year=1996}}</ref><ref name=Bailey1998>{{Cite journal |last1=Bailey |first1=T.A. |last2=Samour |first2=J.H. |last3=Bailey |first3=T.C. |year=1998 |title=Hunted by falcons, protected by falconry: Can the houbara bustard (''Chlamydotis undulata macqueenii'') fly into the 21st century? |journal=Journal of Avian Medicine and Surgery |volume=12 |issue=3 |pages=190–201 |jstor=30133172}}</ref>

Being migratory species, it is important that captive-bred bustards undertake similar migrations as wild birds. Comparing the migrations of captive and wild birds using satellite [[telemetry]], it was found that captive-bred individuals started autumn migration later and wintered closer to the breeding grounds than wild individuals. The surviving captive-bred bustards were also faithful in their wintering locations in subsequent years. As migration has a genetic component, it is important to consider migratory population structure, as well as natal and release-site fidelity, during captive breeding management of this species.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Burnside, R. J. |author2=Collar, N. J. |author3=Dolman, P. M. |year=2017 |title=Comparative migration strategies of wild and captive-bred Asian Houbara ''Chlamydotis macqueenii'' |journal=Ibis |volume=159 |pages=374–389 |doi=10.1111/ibi.12462 |issue=2 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

The Asian houbara is protected in the [[United Arab Emirates]]. In February 2019, 50 individuals were released into the [[Arabian Desert]] in [[Al Ain Region]] in the [[Emirate of Abu Dhabi]], to help conserve the species and increase its numbers in the wild.<ref>{{cite news |work=[[The National (Abu Dhabi)|The National]] |title=Fifty Houbara birds released into the UAE desert - in pictures |date=2019 |url=https://www.thenationalnews.com/uae/environment/fifty-houbara-birds-released-into-the-uae-desert-in-pictures-1.829114 |access-date=2019-02-28}}</ref>

zh.extract:
波斑鴇（Chlamydotis macqueenii）是一種大型的鴇，分佈在亞洲西南部。牠們起初是翎頜鴇的亞種，現被重新分類為獨立的物種。波斑鴇與翎頜鴇的分界位於西奈半島。


特徵
波斑鴇長60厘米，翼展140厘米。上身呈褐色，下身白色，頸側有黑紋。牠們飛行時可以見到翼上的黑色及褐色飛羽。雌鳥較為細小，而上身則較為灰色。牠們不怎麼發聲。波斑鴇較翎頜鴇稍大及淡色。


行為
波斑鴇會豎起頭及喉嚨上的白色羽毛來示愛。牠們每次會生2-4隻蛋。
波斑鴇是雜食性的，主要吃種子、昆蟲及其他細小的生物。


保育
波斑鴇棲息在沙漠及非常乾旱的地區。牠們於中東曾被捕獵至接近滅絕的邊緣，因阿拉伯聯合酋長國的扎耶德·本·蘇爾坦·阿勒納哈揚的保育工作下而得到生存的希望。


參考


外部連結
波斑鴇的分類建議
